does: This paper introduces ODRPO, a framework for robust reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) that addresses noise in LLM-based auto-raters for LLM alignment. The core contribution decomposes multi-tier discrete rewards (e.g., 1-10 rubrics) into ordinal binary indicators, structurally isolating evaluation noise rather than treating it as a monolithic signal. The method demonstrates that this decomposition reduces the corruption of advantage estimators like GRPO and MaxRL, which are sensitive to noisy reward normalization statistics. The authors empirically verify that auto-raters produce stochastic outputs due to prompt sensitivity and sampling randomness, and show that ODRPO achieves better performance than standard approaches and majority voting strategies while remaining computationally efficient, providing a principled alternative to expensive multi-sampling schemes.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and its AI-based variant RLAIF for aligning large language models on subjective, non-verifiable tasks like long-form question answering. It extends prior research on advantage estimation methods in policy gradient algorithms, particularly GRPO and MaxRL, which form the foundation for modern LLM alignment. The paper directly addresses a fundamental challenge identified in reward modeling literature: the unreliability of single-pass evaluations. It connects to ordinal regression and decomposition methods in machine learning, applying these techniques to the specific problem of handling multi-tier discrete rewards. The work also relates to broader efforts in making RL training more robust to noisy signals, building on understanding that normalization-based advantage estimators are vulnerable to reward corruption.
enables: ODRPO opens several future directions for robust LLM alignment. By providing a principled structural approach to noise-robust reward decomposition, it enables more practical RLAIF systems that don't require expensive multi-sampling or majority voting overhead. The framework could extend to other domains using categorical or ordinal feedback signals beyond LLM alignment. Future work might explore adaptive ordinal decomposition strategies that adjust to different reward distributions, or combine ODRPO with uncertainty quantification methods to further improve robustness. The approach also suggests investigating similar decomposition strategies for other forms of noisy feedback signals in RL. Additionally, this work could inform development of better auto-rater designs that exploit the ordinal structure of evaluation criteria, and enable scaling alignment methods to more subjective, fine-grained evaluation rubrics.
